title: Okra Leaf Disease Detection using DeiT and Mistral-7B
description: MSCS research focused on detecting okra leaf diseases using a Data-efficient Image Transformer (DeiT) model and integrating Mistral-7B LLM to generate human-readable explanations of predictions.
problem: Manual identification of okra leaf diseases is time-consuming and requires agricultural expertise. Deep learning models can automate detection, but explanations are often difficult for non-technical users to understand.
methodology: Fine-tuned a pretrained DeiT model for okra leaf disease classification and integrated Mistral-7B to convert model predictions into understandable natural-language explanations.
results: The system supports automated disease classification and generates readable explanations to help farmers, students, and agricultural experts understand model outputs.

title: Few-Shot Learning
description: Developing methods that enable models to learn from very few labeled examples by leveraging meta-learning and transfer learning strategies.
problem: Traditional deep learning systems require massive annotated datasets, which are expensive and difficult to collect in many real-world applications.
methodology: Implemented few-shot learning pipelines using transfer learning and lightweight adaptation strategies to improve data efficiency.
results: Initial experiments demonstrated strong learning capability under limited training samples with promising generalization performance.

title: Vision Transformers
description: Exploring attention-based architectures for image recognition, object detection, and semantic segmentation tasks.
problem: CNN-based architectures face limitations in capturing long-range dependencies and global image context.
methodology: Evaluated DeiT and Vision Transformer architectures for efficient image understanding and classification tasks.
results: Transformer-based models achieved competitive performance with improved feature representation capabilities.

title: Domain Adaptation
description: Bridging the gap between source and target domains to enable robust model deployment across diverse real-world conditions.
problem: AI models trained on one domain often perform poorly when deployed in different environmental conditions.
methodology: Investigated feature alignment and transfer learning techniques to improve cross-domain generalization.
results: Adaptation-based approaches reduced performance degradation between source and target domains.

title: Incremental Learning
description: Building systems that continuously acquire new knowledge without forgetting previously learned information.
problem: Conventional neural networks suffer from catastrophic forgetting when trained on new classes incrementally.
methodology: Designed experiments for class-incremental learning scenarios using memory-efficient learning pipelines.
results: Incremental training strategies showed improved retention of previously learned knowledge.
